title: Shipping Household Goods to the Netherlands
metaTitle: Shipping Household Goods to the Netherlands: Costs, Methods & Tips
subtitle: A practical guide to shipping methods, costs, timelines, customs, and planning your relocation logistics.
description: Learn how expats ship household goods to the Netherlands. Compare sea freight vs air freight, typical costs, timelines, customs considerations, and relocation services.

image.alt: Person with shipping inventory checklist, packed moving boxes and suitcase, with Dutch canal and moving van visible through the window

heroCta.title: Plan your relocation logistics
heroCta.supportingText: Use the Moving Checklist Generator to map out what applies to your move, including shipping, documents, housing, arrival admin, and your first months in the Netherlands.

quickAnswers.label: Who this guide is for
quickAnswers.value: Expats moving personal belongings internationally to the Netherlands.

quickAnswers.label: Common shipping methods
quickAnswers.value: Air freight for speed; sea freight for larger shipments and lower cost per volume.

quickAnswers.label: Best next step
quickAnswers.value: Estimate your logistics early and use the moving checklist tool to avoid delays.

sections.heading: Overview
body: Many expats choose to ship some household goods when relocating to the Netherlands. What makes sense depends on distance, volume, timing, housing type, and whether items are worth replacing locally.

This page explains the usual shipping methods, costs, timelines, customs considerations, and how to plan the logistics. Costs and procedures vary by provider and origin country—use this as a planning guide and confirm details with your moving company and Dutch customs where relevant.

links.label: Documents needed to move to the Netherlands

sections.heading: Shipping methods
body: Most international relocations use one of these methods.
Air freight: faster and higher cost; better for urgent, smaller shipments.
Sea freight: slower but usually better value for larger volumes; common for full relocations and furniture.
Shared container / groupage: lower cost than a full container and more flexible for medium-sized moves; timing can be less predictable.
Courier / box shipping: suitable for suitcases, boxes, documents, and small personal items; often better than full relocation services for light moves.
Choose based on urgency, volume, housing certainty, and whether your items are worth shipping compared with buying again locally.

sections.heading: Typical shipping costs
body: The table below gives indicative planning ranges only. Your actual costs will depend on origin country, volume, customs handling, door-to-door vs port-to-port, packing services, insurance, and final delivery location in the Netherlands. Always compare provider estimates for your exact route and shipment size.

Costs vary depending on: origin country; shipment volume and weight; customs handling; door-to-door vs port-to-port; packing services; insurance; and final delivery location in the Netherlands. These are broad planning ranges, not quotes.

sections.heading: Typical shipping timelines
body: Rough timelines by method: courier or small parcel shipping often takes days to around two weeks; air freight around one to two weeks; shared sea container around four to eight weeks; full sea container around four to ten weeks.
If your housing is temporary or not yet confirmed, avoid timing delivery too early.
bullets: Courier / small parcel: often days to ~2 weeks

callout.title: What often takes longer than expected
callout.text: Customs clearance, consolidating groupage shipments, delivery scheduling after arrival, and waiting for a final address or key handover can add delay. Plan buffer time and confirm with your provider.

sections.heading: Choosing a moving company
body: When comparing providers, consider: whether packing is included, customs documentation support, storage options, delivery model (door-to-door vs port-to-port), insurance availability, and whether quotes are by volume or weight. Check that they serve your origin country.
bullets: Before choosing a provider: request two to four quotes, confirm what is included, ask about customs handling, confirm delivery timing assumptions, and check cancellation or rescheduling rules.

sections.heading: Customs and documents
body: Customs rules vary by route and household situation. Expats often need an inventory list and shipping paperwork. Personal used household goods may be treated differently from new goods or commercial imports. Confirm current requirements with Dutch customs and your shipping provider.
bullets: Common preparation items: passport or ID, proof of relocation or address, inventory list, transport or shipping paperwork, and customs declarations as required by the provider.

callout.title: Important
callout.text: Do not assume all personal belongings can be imported the same way. Customs treatment, exemptions, and required paperwork vary by provider and route. Confirm with Dutch customs and your moving company.

links.label: Documents needed to move to the Netherlands

sections.heading: What expats usually ship
body: Often worth shipping: sentimental items, high-quality furniture, personal equipment, specialist work gear, and items that are expensive to replace locally.
Often not worth shipping: low-value furniture, bulky cheap household items, easily replaceable basics, low-end appliances, and items you may not have room for in Dutch housing.
Dutch homes and apartments can be smaller than people expect, so measure before shipping large furniture.

sections.heading: Receiving shipments
body: Once goods arrive, timing often depends on customs clearance, your delivery address, delivery appointment availability, and building access (lift, parking).
bullets: Confirm your final delivery address with the provider
Check building access rules (stairs, lift, parking)
Keep shipping and customs documents accessible

faq.q: Is it worth shipping furniture to the Netherlands?
faq.a: It depends on the value, size, and sentimental attachment. High-quality or hard-to-replace furniture is often worth shipping; cheap or bulky items may cost more to ship than to replace locally. Dutch homes can be smaller than in some countries, so measure before committing.

faq.q: How long does international shipping to the Netherlands usually take?
faq.a: Courier or small parcels often take a few days to about two weeks; air freight about one to two weeks; shared or full sea containers typically four to ten weeks. Customs and delivery scheduling can add delay.

faq.q: Is air freight or sea freight better for moving?
faq.a: Air freight is faster and suits urgent or smaller moves; sea freight usually offers better value for larger volumes and full household moves. Choose based on urgency, volume, and budget.

faq.q: How much does it cost to ship household goods to the Netherlands?
faq.a: Indicative ranges: courier/suitcase €50–€250 per item; air freight €1,500–€4,000; shared container €2,000–€6,000; full container €5,000–€12,000+. Costs vary by origin, volume, and services; get quotes for your situation.

faq.q: Do I need customs paperwork for personal belongings?
faq.a: Often yes. Requirements vary by route and provider. Commonly needed: inventory list, shipping paperwork, proof of relocation or address, and any customs declarations your provider or Dutch customs require. Confirm with your moving company and Dutch customs.

faq.q: Should I ship furniture or buy new items in the Netherlands?
faq.a: Compare shipping cost and hassle with local replacement cost. Sentimental or high-value items often justify shipping; cheap or bulky items may not. Consider whether your Dutch home has space before shipping large pieces.

faq.q: Can I ship items before I have a permanent address?
faq.a: Some providers offer storage or delivery to a temporary address; others require a final delivery address. Timing delivery before you have keys or a fixed address can lead to delays or extra cost. Confirm options with your provider.
